turns.content: power go out again last night friend. second time this week. aerator stop and i not know until i hear the quiet, you know. too quiet mean something wrong

That's a real problem — when aerators stop, dissolved oxygen drops fast, especially at night when shrimp are most active and there's no photosynthesis happening. How long was the power out before you noticed?
maybe 2 hour. i wake up because something feel wrong. go outside, no noise from aerator. shrimp come to surface already. very dangerous, you know

Two hours is a long time — shrimp surfacing means the oxygen was critically low. You got lucky catching it when you did. At that point you're probably below 3 mg/L dissolved oxygen, which is the danger zone.

Do you have a backup generator? That's really the only reliable solution when power outages are this frequent.
i have old generator but it not start easy anymore. have to pull cord many time. sometime 20 minute just to start. and it make loud noise, neighbor complain

A generator that takes 20 minutes to start basically defeats the purpose in an emergency. A few things to check on the old one:

1. **Spark plug** — if it's fouled or worn, that's the #1 reason for hard starting. Cheap to replace.
2. **Carburetor** — if the generator sits unused for weeks, old fuel gums up the carb. Try draining the old fuel and putting in fresh.
3. **Air filter** — a clogged air filter makes starting really difficult.
4. **Battery** (if it has electric start) — might just need charging.

What brand and roughly how old is the generator?
honda. maybe 8 year old. pull start only, no battery. i change oil last year but not check spark plug before. maybe that is problem

Honda generators are tough — 8 years is nothing for a Honda if it's maintained. The spark plug is almost certainly part of the problem if it's never been changed. After 8 years it'll be corroded and the gap will be wrong.

Here's what I'd do:
1. Replace the spark plug — buy the exact Honda-recommended one, should cost very little
2. Drain all the old fuel from the tank and carburetor, refill with fresh
3. Clean or replace the air filter
4. Try starting it after all three

Also, run the generator for 15-20 minutes every week even when you don't need it. That keeps the fuel system clear and the engine ready.
yes that make sense. i only run when power go out so maybe that why it hard to start. you know, like old man who sit too long then try to stand up ha

Ha, exactly! Same idea. A weekly run keeps everything lubricated and prevents fuel from going stale in the lines.

One more thing — you might want to set up the generator so it's always connected and ready to go. Have the fuel line and electrical connections pre-wired so when power goes out, all you have to do is start it and flip one switch. No fumbling around in the dark at 2am.

pre-wired sound good but i worry about cost, friend. electrician in town charge a lot for that kind of work. and fuel not cheap either, you know. diesel price go up again this month. i pay maybe 22,000 dong per liter now

I understand the cost concern. Let me break it down though:

- The pre-wiring is a one-time cost — once it's done, it's done for years
- Compared to losing shrimp from a power outage, the electrician fee is probably a lot less. How much did that 2-hour outage cost you in stressed or lost shrimp?

As for diesel at 22,000 dong per liter — a typical backup generator running your aerators might burn 1-2 liters per hour. If you run it maybe 5-10 hours a month during outages, that's 110,000 to 220,000 dong in fuel. Compare that to the value of the shrimp you're protecting.

But I get it — everything adds up. The spark plug and air filter fix costs almost nothing and should solve the hard-starting problem first. Start there.

you right. last time power out 2 hour i think maybe lose some shrimp from stress. hard to count exactly but next morning some float. maybe 3-4 kilo dead. at market price that is real money gone, you know

Exactly — 3-4 kg of dead shrimp at current market prices probably costs you more than a whole year's worth of generator fuel and maintenance combined. And that's just the dead ones — the stressed ones that survived might grow slower for weeks after.

So the math actually works in your favor:
- Spark plug + air filter: very cheap, maybe 50,000-100,000 dong
- Fresh fuel: you need to buy diesel anyway
- Weekly 15-minute run: maybe 0.5 liter = 11,000 dong per week
- Pre-wiring (eventually): one-time cost

Versus losing 3-4 kg of shrimp every outage, plus growth slowdown on the survivors. The generator maintenance pays for itself the first time it starts on the first pull.

when you say number like that i understand, friend. number never lie. okay but what about the noise problem? generator very loud at night. my neighbor house maybe 30 meter away. he come yell at me last time at 3am ha

Ha, I can imagine that wasn't a fun conversation at 3am. A few things that can help with generator noise:

1. **Build a simple enclosure** — even a three-sided shed with a roof, lined with plywood, will cut the noise a lot. Just make sure you leave ventilation so the engine doesn't overheat and exhaust can escape.
2. **Rubber mat underneath** — the vibration against the ground makes a lot of the noise. A thick rubber mat or old tires cut the vibration.
3. **Point the exhaust away** from the neighbor's house — aim it toward open water or field instead.
4. **Distance** — even moving it 10-15 meters further from the neighbor helps a lot. Sound drops off fast with distance.

You don't need to buy a new quiet generator — just reducing vibration and putting up a barrier makes a big difference.

enclosure i can build from old wood, you know. i have plenty wood from when i fix pond bank last month. rubber mat i also have — old tire from truck. that is free. only problem is moving generator far from pond. cable too short

The cable problem has an easy fix — you can get a heavy-duty extension cord rated for outdoor use. Make sure it's thick enough gauge to handle the load from your aerators without overheating. A 10-15 meter heavy-duty cord shouldn't be too expensive.

Or, if the electrician does the pre-wiring later, he can run a longer permanent cable from wherever you park the generator. That's actually the better long-term solution.

For now though: old wood enclosure + rubber tire mat underneath + exhaust pointed away from the neighbor. All free materials you already have. Your neighbor will still hear it, but it won't shake his windows at 3am anymore.

okay friend. i make plan now. tomorrow morning buy spark plug and air filter first. this weekend i build wood enclosure behind pond shed, put rubber tire underneath. and i start running generator every week like you say. maybe sunday morning when neighbor already awake ha

Ha, Sunday morning is a good plan — no angry visits at 3am! That's a solid maintenance schedule:

- **Tomorrow:** spark plug + air filter
- **Weekend:** build the enclosure, set up the rubber mat
- **Every Sunday:** 15-minute test run to keep it ready

Once the spark plug is fresh and the fuel system is clean, I bet that Honda fires up on the first or second pull. Eight years old is nothing for those engines when they're maintained. Good luck, and let me know if the starting problem goes away after the new plug!

thank you friend. power company here very bad, you know. every rainy season same story — power go out, come back, go out again. i cannot depend on them. have to depend on myself. that is how it always been for farmer. okay i go check fuel level now before dark. bye bye

That's the farmer's way — depend on yourself first, because nobody else will keep your shrimp alive at 2am. Good luck with the spark plug tomorrow, and stay on top of that weekly schedule. Your aerators — and your shrimp — will thank you for it!
